title: Houses for Rent in Cleveland, OH Under $1,000
metaTitle: Houses for Rent in Cleveland OH Under $1,000
metaDescription: Houses for rent in Cleveland, OH under $1,000/mo from a local property manager — realistic sizes and neighborhoods, most Section 8-friendly. Book a showing.
primaryKeyword: houses for rent cleveland ohio under 1000
answerBox: Houses for rent in Cleveland, OH under $1,000 a month are realistic mainly for 1- and 2-bedroom homes; 3-bedroom homes typically start just above $1,000. Under-$1,000 rentals are common on the city's East and Southeast sides, and most accept Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8).

sections.h2: Can you actually rent a house under $1,000 in Cleveland?
body: Yes, but honestly, it depends on bedroom count. Across our own portfolio, rents run from about $700 to $1,800/mo with a typical rent near $1,000 — so under-$1,000 is realistic for smaller homes, but it isn't a given for every size. 1-bedroom and most 2-bedroom homes commonly fall under $1,000; 3-bedroom and larger homes typically start right around $1,100 and up.

For context, Zumper's July 4, 2026 report puts Cleveland's citywide median asking rent at $1,250/mo overall, with a 2-bedroom median of $1,100/mo — both above the $1,000 mark. That means an under-$1,000 house is often at the lower end of the local market, not the typical price.

sections.h2: What's realistic under $1,000, by bedroom count
body: Here's a realistic picture based on current availability across our own homes. Treat the top row as the most reliable bet if a strict $1,000 ceiling matters to you.

rows: Often, especially at the lower end
No — larger homes run well above $1,000

sections.h2: Where under-$1,000 houses are most available
body: Our most affordable inventory is concentrated on Cleveland's East and Southeast sides — Slavic Village (44105), Collinwood (44110), Glenville (44108), Fairfax/Central (44104), and Hough (44103) — with a smaller number of homes on the West side around Cudell/Detroit-Shoreway (44102) and Old Brooklyn (44109). These areas are reachable by GCRTA's bus network, and several connect to downtown via the Red Line or the HealthLine on Euclid Avenue.

See the full [houses-for-rent hub](/houses-for-rent-cleveland-oh/) for every neighborhood and bedroom-count option, or go straight to [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) to see what's open at your budget right now.

sections.h2: Stretching an under-$1,000 budget with a voucher
body: If you hold a Housing Choice Voucher, your actual out-of-pocket rent may be lower than the sticker price, since [CMHA](https://www.cmha.net/housing/applicants/housing_choice_vouchers.php) — the Cuyahoga County housing authority — pays a subsidy up to a set payment standard for your bedroom size and covers part of the rent directly. Payment standards change periodically, so check CMHA's current chart rather than assuming a fixed number. Every home we manage accepts vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready; see [HUD's Housing Choice Voucher overview](https://www.hud.gov/helping-americans/housing-choice-vouchers) for how the program works, or our [Section 8 housing guide](/section-8-housing-cleveland-oh/) for local specifics.

sections.h2: What to watch for at this price point
body: At any price under $1,000, always confirm which utilities are included in the rent — some homes bundle water or trash, but most do not include electric or gas, and that difference can matter as much as the sticker rent itself. Our [utilities-included guide](/cleveland-rentals/guides/what-utilities-included-cleveland-rent/) breaks down what's typical for Cleveland rentals. Because affordable inventory moves quickly, the fastest way to see current under-$1,000 options is to [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) or [apply](/apply) once you find a home you like.

It also helps to stay flexible on bedroom count and exact neighborhood if a firm $1,000 ceiling is the priority. A smaller 2-bedroom in one of our East-side ZIPs will typically beat that budget more reliably than holding out for a specific 3-bedroom floor plan, since larger homes are the ones most likely to price above $1,000 regardless of location.

faq.q: Are there houses for rent in Cleveland under $1,000 a month?
faq.a: Yes, especially 1- and 2-bedroom homes, which commonly fall in the $700-$1,100 range. 3-bedroom and larger homes typically start just above $1,000, so a strict under-$1,000 budget is most realistic for smaller households.

faq.q: What size house can I get for under $1,000 in Cleveland?
faq.a: Most often a 1-bedroom or smaller 2-bedroom home. Zumper's July 4, 2026 report shows Cleveland's citywide median 2-bedroom rent at $1,100/mo, so under-$1,000 2-bedrooms tend to be at the lower end of condition or size, not the typical unit.

faq.q: Which Cleveland neighborhoods have the most under-$1,000 houses?
faq.a: Affordable homes under $1,000 are common across East and Southeast side neighborhoods including Slavic Village, Collinwood, Glenville, Fairfax/Central, and Hough, plus a smaller number on the West side in Cudell/Detroit-Shoreway and Old Brooklyn.

faq.q: Do under-$1,000 houses in Cleveland accept Section 8 vouchers?
faq.a: Every home we manage accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready, regardless of the listed rent. With a voucher, CMHA's payment standard may cover part of the rent, potentially reducing what you pay out of pocket below the sticker price.

faq.q: Are utilities included in under-$1,000 rentals?
faq.a: It varies by property. Some homes include water or trash pickup, but most do not include electric or gas. Always confirm exactly what's included before signing, since utility costs can add a meaningful amount to your real monthly housing cost.
